title: Warfarin-Associated Intracerebral Hemorrhage with Intraventricular Extension

authoring_notes.summary: 71-year-old male on warfarin for atrial fibrillation presents after being found down with right-sided weakness and global aphasia. INR is supratherapeutic at 3.2. CT head reveals a large left basal ganglia hemorrhage with intraventricular extension and midline shift. The case requires urgent anticoagulation reversal with 4-factor PCC and IV vitamin K, aggressive blood pressure management targeting SBP <140, neurosurgical consultation, and avoidance of catastrophic errors such as administering tPA without imaging.
teaching_focus: Recognize intracerebral hemorrhage and differentiate from ischemic stroke — CT before any treatment
Emergent reversal of warfarin anticoagulation with 4-factor PCC plus IV vitamin K per AHA/ASA guidelines
Blood pressure management targeting SBP <140 mmHg per INTERACT2 and ATACH-2 trial evidence
Calculate and interpret the ICH Score for prognostication
Serial GCS assessment and recognition of herniation signs (Cushing reflex, unilateral pupil dilation)
Indications for neurosurgical intervention including external ventricular drain (EVD) for hydrocephalus
Avoid tPA, antiplatelets, and other agents that worsen hemorrhage
Seizure prophylaxis considerations in supratentorial ICH
4-factor PCC advantages over FFP: faster INR correction, lower volume, no blood type matching needed

case_metadata.title: Warfarin-Associated Intracerebral Hemorrhage with Intraventricular Extension

monitor.ecg_text: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response, rate 82, no acute ST changes, no STEMI criteria

hidden_truth.final_diagnosis: Warfarin-associated left basal ganglia intracerebral hemorrhage with intraventricular extension and midline shift, in the setting of supratherapeutic anticoagulation (INR 3.2) and uncontrolled hypertension
hidden_truth.head_ct_result_if_ordered: Large left basal ganglia hyperdense lesion measuring approximately 4.5 x 3.2 x 3.0 cm (estimated volume ~45 mL by ABC/2 method). Intraventricular extension of hemorrhage into the left lateral ventricle with early hydrocephalus. Midline shift of 7 mm to the right. Surrounding vasogenic edema. No underlying mass lesion identified. No subarachnoid hemorrhage.
hidden_truth.cta_head_result_if_ordered: No contrast extravasation (spot sign negative). No underlying vascular malformation or aneurysm identified. Atherosclerotic changes in the intracranial vasculature.
hidden_truth.mri_brain_result_if_ordered: Large left basal ganglia hemorrhage with intraventricular extension, consistent with hypertensive hemorrhage. No underlying mass or vascular malformation. Chronic small vessel ischemic changes.
hidden_truth.cxr_if_ordered: Mild cardiomegaly. No acute pulmonary process. No pulmonary edema.
hidden_truth.ecg_if_ordered: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response at 82 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. No acute ST-T wave changes.
hidden_truth.repeat_inr_after_ffp_if_ordered: INR 1.9 (drawn 60 minutes after completion of FFP infusion — correction incomplete and delayed)
hidden_truth.ua_if_ordered: Trace protein, trace glucose, otherwise unremarkable
hidden_truth.neurosurgery_consult_response: Neurosurgery aware. Given ICH score of 4, large hematoma volume with IVH, and midline shift, recommend EVD placement for IVH/hydrocephalus management. Will evaluate for possible surgical evacuation depending on clinical trajectory. Continue aggressive medical management including anticoagulation reversal and BP control. Repeat CT in 6 hours or sooner if clinical deterioration.

opening_script.family_voice: I found him on the floor next to the bed about 20 minutes ago. He was fine when I went to the kitchen — that was maybe 45 minutes ago. He can't talk and he can't move his right side. He takes warfarin for his heart rhythm. Please help him.
opening_script.nurse_handoff: 71-year-old male brought in by EMS after being found down by his wife with right-sided weakness and inability to speak. Last known well approximately 45 minutes ago. History of atrial fibrillation on warfarin, hypertension, diabetes, and coronary artery disease. Vitals: BP 208/118, HR 82 and irregular, RR 18, SpO2 96% on room air. GCS is 10 — eyes open to voice, incomprehensible sounds, localizes to pain on the left but no movement on the right. Right-sided facial droop, dense right hemiplegia, gaze deviation to the left. He is not following commands.
appearance: Elderly male lying on stretcher, not moving right side
Eyes open to voice, gaze deviated to the left
Incomprehensible sounds only — no intelligible speech
Right arm and leg flaccid, no withdrawal to pain on right
No signs of trauma or external bleeding

critical_actions.description: Perform primary survey with ABCs, assess GCS, and evaluate airway protective reflexes
Order emergent non-contrast CT head to differentiate hemorrhagic from ischemic stroke before any treatment
Reverse warfarin anticoagulation with IV vitamin K 10 mg AND 4-factor PCC (dose based on INR and weight) — do not use tPA
Initiate IV antihypertensive (nicardipine infusion or labetalol) targeting SBP <140 mmHg per AHA/ASA ICH guidelines
Order stat coagulation studies including INR, PT, PTT, fibrinogen, CBC with platelets
Consult neurosurgery for evaluation of possible surgical evacuation and/or EVD placement given IVH and hydrocephalus
Consider seizure prophylaxis (levetiracetam) for supratentorial ICH — assess risk vs benefit
Arrange ICU admission for continuous monitoring, titrated BP management, and serial neurologic assessments

harm_events.description: tPA (alteplase) given — catastrophic error; patient has intracerebral hemorrhage and tPA will massively expand the hematoma, likely resulting in herniation and death
harm_events.consequence: Massive hematoma expansion, uncal herniation, and likely death. This is the single most dangerous error in acute stroke management — CT must precede any thrombolytic.
harm_events.teaching_point: Never administer thrombolytics without first obtaining a non-contrast CT head. ICH is an absolute contraindication to tPA. The mantra is: CT before tPA, always.

harm_events.description: Failure to reverse warfarin anticoagulation — ongoing coagulopathy leads to progressive hematoma expansion
harm_events.consequence: Continued hematoma expansion due to uncorrected coagulopathy. Hematoma volume increases by 30-40%, worsening midline shift and neurologic decline.
harm_events.teaching_point: AHA/ASA guidelines recommend emergent reversal of anticoagulation in warfarin-associated ICH. 4-factor PCC corrects INR within minutes vs hours for FFP, and requires much less volume.

harm_events.description: Antiplatelet agent (aspirin, clopidogrel, etc.) given — worsens hemorrhage by impairing platelet aggregation
harm_events.consequence: Further impairment of hemostasis, contributing to hematoma expansion.
harm_events.teaching_point: Antiplatelet agents are contraindicated in acute ICH. Even in patients with coronary artery disease, antiplatelets must be held during active intracranial hemorrhage.

harm_events.description: Failure to lower blood pressure — sustained severe hypertension promotes ongoing hemorrhage and hematoma expansion
harm_events.consequence: Sustained hypertension drives continued bleeding and hematoma expansion, increasing midline shift and herniation risk.
harm_events.teaching_point: INTERACT2 trial showed that intensive BP lowering to SBP <140 improved functional outcomes. IV nicardipine or labetalol are first-line agents for BP management in ICH.

harm_events.description: SBP dropped aggressively below 100 mmHg — cerebral hypoperfusion in the setting of impaired autoregulation and mass effect
harm_events.consequence: Cerebral hypoperfusion leads to secondary ischemic injury in penumbral tissue surrounding the hematoma. Worsening neurologic status.
harm_events.teaching_point: While SBP <140 is the target, overly aggressive lowering is harmful. Cerebral autoregulation is impaired in ICH, and perfusion pressure must be maintained. ATACH-2 showed that targeting SBP 110-139 vs 140-179 did not improve outcomes and increased renal adverse events.

recommended_management_logic.preferred_strategy: Immediate ABCs and GCS, emergent non-contrast CT head, simultaneous coagulation reversal with 4-factor PCC (25-50 units/kg based on INR) plus IV vitamin K 10 mg, aggressive but controlled BP reduction targeting SBP <140 with nicardipine infusion, neurosurgery consultation, serial neuro exams, and ICU admission

anticoagulation_reversal_protocol.four_factor_pcc_dose: For INR 2-4: 25 units/kg (actual body weight). For Harold Brown at 88 kg with INR 3.2: approximately 2200 units IV infused over 10-15 minutes
anticoagulation_reversal_protocol.vitamin_k_dose: 10 mg IV infused over 10-20 minutes (slow infusion to reduce anaphylactoid risk)
anticoagulation_reversal_protocol.monitoring: Recheck INR 15-30 minutes after PCC administration. Target INR <1.4. If INR remains elevated, consider additional PCC dose.
anticoagulation_reversal_protocol.ffp_alternative: If PCC unavailable: FFP 10-15 mL/kg (approximately 4 units). Note: FFP is inferior due to large volume, longer preparation/infusion time, need for blood type matching, and risk of TACO/TRALI.

bp_management_protocol.target: SBP <140 mmHg but not below 110 mmHg
bp_management_protocol.nicardipine: Start at 5 mg/hr IV infusion, titrate by 2.5 mg/hr every 5-15 minutes, max 15 mg/hr
bp_management_protocol.labetalol_alternative: 10-20 mg IV push over 2 minutes, may repeat every 10-20 minutes, max 300 mg total. Consider infusion at 2-8 mg/min.
bp_management_protocol.avoid: Sublingual nifedipine, oral agents only, sodium nitroprusside (raises ICP)

untreated_progression.narrative: The patient becomes less responsive. He no longer opens his eyes to voice — only to pain. His right side remains completely flaccid. The nurse notes the blood pressure has climbed to 215/122.

GCS has dropped to 7. He has no verbal output and only withdraws to pain. The left pupil appears slightly larger and sluggishly reactive. Heart rate is trending down — early Cushing response may be developing. The coagulopathy is uncorrected and the hematoma is expanding.

The patient is now comatose. The left pupil is fixed and dilated at 6 mm — uncal herniation is occurring. Cushing triad is developing: hypertension 235/130, bradycardia at 55, and irregular respirations at 10/min. SpO2 has dropped to 92%. Airway protective reflexes are failing. This patient needs immediate intubation and emergent neurosurgical intervention.
Bilateral fixed and dilated pupils. GCS 3. The patient has herniated. Agonal respirations. Hemodynamic collapse. Without aggressive intervention at this point, the outcome is fatal.

treated_progression.narrative: After 4-factor PCC and nicardipine infusion, the INR has corrected to 1.3. Blood pressure is trending down to 165/95. GCS remains stable at 10. The risk of hematoma expansion is markedly reduced.
Blood pressure is at target with SBP 138. Neurologic exam is stable. Neurosurgery has been consulted and is evaluating for EVD placement. The patient is being prepared for ICU transfer.

head_ct_ordered.result_text: Large left basal ganglia hyperdense lesion measuring approximately 4.5 x 3.2 x 3.0 cm (estimated volume ~45 mL by ABC/2 method). Intraventricular extension of hemorrhage into the left lateral ventricle and third ventricle with early obstructive hydrocephalus. Midline shift of 7 mm to the right. Surrounding vasogenic edema with effacement of the left lateral ventricle. No subarachnoid hemorrhage. No skull fracture.

cta_head_neck_ordered.result_text: No spot sign (contrast extravasation) identified. No underlying aneurysm or vascular malformation. Moderate intracranial atherosclerotic disease. Patent carotid and vertebral arteries bilaterally.

inr_stat_ordered.result_text: INR 3.2, PT 38.4 seconds (reference 11.0-13.5). PTT 42 seconds (reference 25-35). Supratherapeutic anticoagulation.

repeat_inr_post_pcc_ordered.result_text: INR 1.3. Adequate reversal achieved with 4-factor PCC.

cbc_ordered.result_text: WBC 9.8 K/uL, Hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL, Hematocrit 39.3%, Platelets 178 K/uL. No thrombocytopenia.

cmp_ordered.result_text: Na 141, K 4.1, Cl 103, CO2 24, BUN 22, Creatinine 1.2, Glucose 168, Calcium 9.1. Mild hyperglycemia. Renal function near baseline.

troponin_ordered.result_text: Troponin I 8 ng/L (reference <26 ng/L). Normal, no evidence of acute myocardial injury.

ecg_ordered.result_text: Atrial fibrillation with controlled ventricular response, rate 82 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. No acute ST-T wave changes. No STEMI.

cxr_ordered.result_text: Mild cardiomegaly. Clear lung fields. No pulmonary edema, infiltrate, or effusion. Endotracheal tube in satisfactory position if intubated.

blood_type_and_screen_ordered.result_text: Blood type O positive. Antibody screen negative.

abg_ordered.result_text: pH 7.42, pCO2 36 mmHg, pO2 85 mmHg, HCO3 23 mEq/L, BE -1. No acid-base disturbance. Mild hypoxia on room air.

ua_ordered.result_text: Trace protein, trace glucose, otherwise unremarkable. No hematuria.

repeat_ct_head_ordered.result_text_if_treated: Left basal ganglia hemorrhage appears stable in size compared to prior. Midline shift unchanged at 7 mm. IVH stable. No new hemorrhage.
repeat_ct_head_ordered.result_text_if_untreated: Left basal ganglia hemorrhage has expanded significantly, now measuring approximately 6.0 x 4.5 x 4.0 cm (estimated volume ~72 mL). Increased midline shift now 12 mm. Increased IVH with blood layering in all ventricles. Worsening hydrocephalus. Evolving uncal herniation.

what_went_well: Rapidly assessed ABCs and GCS on arrival
Obtained emergent CT head before initiating any stroke-specific therapy
Correctly identified intracerebral hemorrhage and withheld tPA
Reversed anticoagulation promptly with 4-factor PCC and IV vitamin K
Achieved target SBP <140 with titratable IV antihypertensive
Obtained coagulation studies and monitored INR correction
Consulted neurosurgery early for surgical evaluation
Performed serial neurologic assessments and monitored for herniation
what_could_have_gone_better: Faster time to CT head — goal is door-to-CT <25 minutes
Earlier anticoagulation reversal — 4-factor PCC should be ordered as soon as warfarin-associated ICH is suspected
More frequent blood pressure monitoring and faster titration to target
Earlier neurosurgery notification even before CT confirmation if high clinical suspicion
Consideration of airway protection given GCS ≤8 risk and declining mental status
Glucose management — hyperglycemia worsens outcomes in ICH
Blood glucose check and tight glucose control targeting 140-180 mg/dL
key_choices_to_highlight: Was CT obtained before any stroke-specific therapy? This is the most critical decision point.
Was 4-factor PCC chosen over FFP, and was vitamin K given concurrently?
What BP target was chosen and how quickly was it achieved?
Was the antihypertensive agent titratable (nicardipine infusion vs repeated boluses)?
Were serial neurologic exams performed to monitor for deterioration?
Was neurosurgery consulted, and was EVD placement considered for IVH/hydrocephalus?
Was seizure prophylaxis considered for this supratentorial hemorrhage?
Was the airway reassessed as GCS potentially declined?
teaching_points: CT head before any treatment is the cardinal rule of acute stroke management — hemorrhagic and ischemic strokes are clinically indistinguishable, and tPA in ICH is catastrophic.

AHA/ASA guidelines recommend 4-factor PCC over FFP for warfarin reversal in ICH: PCC corrects INR in minutes (vs hours for FFP), requires minimal volume (~100 mL vs 1000+ mL for FFP), does not require blood type matching, and has lower risk of TACO/TRALI.
IV vitamin K (10 mg slow infusion) should always accompany PCC because PCC factors have a shorter half-life than warfarin — without vitamin K, the INR will re-elevate in 12-24 hours.

INTERACT2 trial demonstrated that intensive BP lowering to SBP <140 within 1 hour improved functional outcomes. ATACH-2 showed that targeting SBP 110-139 vs 140-179 did not add benefit and increased renal adverse events — avoid going below SBP 110.

The ICH Score (0-6) uses GCS, age, ICH volume, IVH presence, and infratentorial origin to estimate 30-day mortality. This patient's ICH Score of 4 carries an estimated 97% 30-day mortality — goals of care discussion is essential.

Hematoma expansion occurs in ~30% of ICH patients within the first few hours and is the most important modifiable predictor of poor outcome. Anticoagulation reversal and BP control are the two key interventions to prevent expansion.

Signs of herniation (Cushing triad: hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations; unilateral fixed dilated pupil; decerebrate posturing) require immediate intervention: HOB elevation, osmotic therapy (mannitol or hypertonic saline), hyperventilation as a bridge, and emergent neurosurgical decompression.
EVD (external ventricular drain) is indicated for ICH with IVH causing hydrocephalus, particularly when GCS is declining. It allows CSF drainage and ICP monitoring.
Sodium nitroprusside should be avoided in ICH because its metabolite (cyanide) can raise intracranial pressure.
The 'stroke mimic' concept works both ways: not all focal deficits are ischemic strokes, and treatment decisions must be guided by imaging, not clinical impression alone.
